item.title:
TALKS PROGRESS, STRIKES DELAYED
Trump: “The United States and Iran have had productive discussions over the past two days toward fully resolving hostilities in the Middle East. As talks continue this week, I’ve ordered a five-day pause on any military strikes against Iranian energy infrastructure, contingent on progress. Thank you. —President Donald J. Trump”
item.description:
TALKS PROGRESS, STRIKES DELAYED

Trump: “The United States and Iran have had productive discussions over the past two days toward fully resolving hostilities in the Middle East. As talks continue this week, I’ve ordered a five-day pause on any military strikes against Iranian energy infrastructure, contingent on progress. Thank you. —President Donald J. Trump”

item.description: ISRAEL MILITARY SPOKESMAN: WE EXPECT 'WEEKS' MORE OF FIGHTING AGAINST IRAN AND HEZBOLLAH

item.title:
NATO VOWS TO REOPEN HORMUZ
NATO chief Mark Rutte said he is “absolutely convinced” the alliance will reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
He noted allies are coordinating their response after weeks of planning, stressing the importance of acting together with the U.S.
Rutte also called the U.S. operation “crucial,” citing Iran as an “existential threat.”
item.description:
NATO VOWS TO REOPEN HORMUZ

NATO chief Mark Rutte said he is “absolutely convinced” the alliance will reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

He noted allies are coordinating their response after weeks of planning, stressing the importance of acting together with the U.S.

Rutte also called the U.S. operation “crucial,” citing Iran as an “existential threat.”

item.title:
IRAN MISSILES COULD REACH EUROPE
U.S. Ambassador Michael Waltz warned Iran is developing long-range missiles capable of hitting Europe, possibly hidden within its “space program.”
He said the real risk is combining that capability with a nuclear warhead—and stressed the U.S. is acting before it happens.
Waltz also reiterated President Trump’s warning: reopen the Strait of Hormuz within 48 hours or face strikes on major power infrastructure.
item.description:
IRAN MISSILES COULD REACH EUROPE

U.S. Ambassador Michael Waltz warned Iran is developing long-range missiles capable of hitting Europe, possibly hidden within its “space program.”

He said the real risk is combining that capability with a nuclear warhead—and stressed the U.S. is acting before it happens.

Waltz also reiterated President Trump’s warning: reopen the Strait of Hormuz within 48 hours or face strikes on major power infrastructure.

item.title:
📈RECESSION FEARS RISE AGAIN
Recession odds have jumped to 36.9%, rebounding sharply after months of decline.
Markets are signaling rising uncertainty—potentially driven by geopolitical tensions and a broader risk-off mood. https://kalshi.com/markets/kxrecssnber/recession/kxrecssnber-26?utm_source=walterbloomberg
item.description:
📈RECESSION FEARS RISE AGAIN

Recession odds have jumped to 36.9%, rebounding sharply after months of decline.

Markets are signaling rising uncertainty—potentially driven by geopolitical tensions and a broader risk-off mood.
kalshi.com/markets/kxrecssnb…

item.title:
BITCOIN SLUMPS AS WAR FEARS SHAKE CRYPTO
Bitcoin fell below $69,000, hitting its lowest level since early March, as rising tensions between the U.S., Israel, and Iran rattled markets.
The cryptocurrency is down about 20% since the conflict began, undermining its reputation as a safe-haven asset. Other tokens dropped harder, with Ether sliding nearly 5% and several major altcoins also declining.
Analysts say the selloff reflects broader risk aversion, with stocks and crypto both under pressure, alongside rising energy costs that make mining more expensive.
With oil prices climbing and geopolitical risks intensifying, investors are pulling back from riskier assets—keeping crypto markets under strain.
item.description:
BITCOIN SLUMPS AS WAR FEARS SHAKE CRYPTO

Bitcoin fell below $69,000, hitting its lowest level since early March, as rising tensions between the U.S., Israel, and Iran rattled markets.

The cryptocurrency is down about 20% since the conflict began, undermining its reputation as a safe-haven asset. Other tokens dropped harder, with Ether sliding nearly 5% and several major altcoins also declining.

Analysts say the selloff reflects broader risk aversion, with stocks and crypto both under pressure, alongside rising energy costs that make mining more expensive.

With oil prices climbing and geopolitical risks intensifying, investors are pulling back from riskier assets—keeping crypto markets under strain.

item.title:
HORMUZ OPEN—BUT NOT FOR IRAN’S ENEMIES
Iran says ships can pass through the Strait of Hormuz only if they coordinate with Tehran and are not linked to its adversaries. Officials reiterated that the U.S., Israel, and their allies are excluded.
Traffic through the strait has dropped sharply—from about 100 weekly transits before the conflict to just seven now, mostly Iranian oil tankers.
Security risks remain high. A bulk carrier reported an explosion near the UAE coast, and Iran is reportedly charging some vessels a $2 million transit fee, though this claim is unverified.
item.description:
HORMUZ OPEN—BUT NOT FOR IRAN’S ENEMIES

Iran says ships can pass through the Strait of Hormuz only if they coordinate with Tehran and are not linked to its adversaries. Officials reiterated that the U.S., Israel, and their allies are excluded.

Traffic through the strait has dropped sharply—from about 100 weekly transits before the conflict to just seven now, mostly Iranian oil tankers.

Security risks remain high. A bulk carrier reported an explosion near the UAE coast, and Iran is reportedly charging some vessels a $2 million transit fee, though this claim is unverified.

item.description: US REJECTED PUTIN OFFER FOR IRAN, UKRAINE QUID PRO QUO: POLTICO

item.title:
PUTIN OFFERS TO STOP INTEL TO IRAN IF US CUTS UKRAINE
Russia proposed to the U.S. that it would halt sharing intelligence with Iran if Washington stopped providing intel to Ukraine. The offer, made by envoy Kirill Dmitriev to Trump aides in Miami, was rejected.
Europeans worry Moscow aims to split the U.S. from its allies. Russia has increased intelligence and military support to Iran, despite calling reports “fake news.”
The Kremlin said Ukraine peace talks are “on hold,” while the U.S. continues limited intelligence sharing and weapons deliveries to Kyiv. Recent easing of Russian oil sanctions has further alarmed European leaders.
item.description:
PUTIN OFFERS TO STOP INTEL TO IRAN IF US CUTS UKRAINE

Russia proposed to the U.S. that it would halt sharing intelligence with Iran if Washington stopped providing intel to Ukraine. The offer, made by envoy Kirill Dmitriev to Trump aides in Miami, was rejected.

Europeans worry Moscow aims to split the U.S. from its allies. Russia has increased intelligence and military support to Iran, despite calling reports “fake news.”

The Kremlin said Ukraine peace talks are “on hold,” while the U.S. continues limited intelligence sharing and weapons deliveries to Kyiv. Recent easing of Russian oil sanctions has further alarmed European leaders.

item.title:
TRUMP HAS NO PLANS TO SEND TROOPS INTO IRAN, WHITE HOUSE SAYS
A White House official said Trump has “no plans” to deploy troops to Iran despite new troop movements and reports of a possible Kharg Island blockade. The official added the U.S. military could “take out Kharg Island at any time” but is keeping options open and not revealing strategy.
item.description:
TRUMP HAS NO PLANS TO SEND TROOPS INTO IRAN, WHITE HOUSE SAYS

A White House official said Trump has “no plans” to deploy troops to Iran despite new troop movements and reports of a possible Kharg Island blockade. The official added the U.S. military could “take out Kharg Island at any time” but is keeping options open and not revealing strategy.

item.title:
UBS RAISES OIL FORECASTS FOR 2026-27 AMID MIDDLE EAST TENSIONS
UBS lifted its oil price forecasts for 2026-27 following the Strait of Hormuz closure and ongoing Middle East conflict.
The bank now expects $86 per barrel in 2026 (up $14) and $80 in 2027 (up $10). Analysts assume the conflict lasts another 2–3 weeks, keeping flows through the Strait sharply reduced, with gradual recovery starting in April but no full normalization.
item.description:
UBS RAISES OIL FORECASTS FOR 2026-27 AMID MIDDLE EAST TENSIONS

UBS lifted its oil price forecasts for 2026-27 following the Strait of Hormuz closure and ongoing Middle East conflict.

The bank now expects $86 per barrel in 2026 (up $14) and $80 in 2027 (up $10). Analysts assume the conflict lasts another 2–3 weeks, keeping flows through the Strait sharply reduced, with gradual recovery starting in April but no full normalization.

item.title: IRGC'S QUDS FORCE COMMANDER QAANI LAUDS 'RESISTANCE FRONT FOR FIGHTING U.S., ISRAEL; SUPPORTING IRAN IN WAR - IRANIAN MEDIA
item.description: IRGC'S QUDS FORCE COMMANDER QAANI LAUDS 'RESISTANCE FRONT FOR FIGHTING U.S., ISRAEL; SUPPORTING IRAN IN WAR - IRANIAN MEDIA

item.title:
TRADERS BOOST FED HIKE BETS TO 50% AS YIELDS SPIKE
US Treasury yields jumped after traders increased bets on a Federal Reserve rate hike to 50% by October, citing inflation risks from the ongoing Middle East conflict.
Yields rose 9–13 basis points across maturities, with two-year notes most sensitive to policy. Five-year yields topped 4% for the first time since July, and the 10-year climbed to 4.33%, the highest since August.
Money markets have shifted from expecting two rate cuts this year to pricing in possible hikes. “The Treasury market is worried about rising inflation as the Iran conflict escalates,” said Gennadiy Goldberg, TD Securities’ US rates strategist.
item.description:
TRADERS BOOST FED HIKE BETS TO 50% AS YIELDS SPIKE

US Treasury yields jumped after traders increased bets on a Federal Reserve rate hike to 50% by October, citing inflation risks from the ongoing Middle East conflict.

Yields rose 9–13 basis points across maturities, with two-year notes most sensitive to policy. Five-year yields topped 4% for the first time since July, and the 10-year climbed to 4.33%, the highest since August.

Money markets have shifted from expecting two rate cuts this year to pricing in possible hikes. “The Treasury market is worried about rising inflation as the Iran conflict escalates,” said Gennadiy Goldberg, TD Securities’ US rates strategist.
